nativeRange.habitat: Lori's Anthias is typically found in small groups on steep outer reef drop-offs, near caves and ledges at depths of 30 to 60 m, though the species has been recorded anywhere from 7 to 70 m.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and ledges

diet.notes: Feeds on crustacean larvae and fish eggs in the wild. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and over time high-quality flake foods. A refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is generally considered beneficial to provide a steady supply of live zooplankton. Multiple small feedings per day are advisable given the species' high metabolism.

avoidWith: other anthias males in small systems

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Izuzuki. 640x480. Sharp in-water photo of a live, vibrant individual clearly showing the diagnostic pink-red body with orange tiger-like bars along the upper rear flank and green eye; whole fish well framed.

Photographer John E. Randall (SAIAB via GBIF). 600x400. Live in-situ reef-cave shot showing the red body, elongated fins and rear tiger striping; slightly soft/hazy so kept as a non-recommended habitat complement.

summary: Lori's Anthias (Pseudanthias lori) is a deep-water Indo-Pacific reef fish generally considered more docile than many other anthias species. It is best kept as a harem group — one male with several females — in a reef aquarium with caves, ledges, and frequent small feedings.
careNotes:
Lori's Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual in a group will transition to male over time. Keeping a group of three or more (one male with two or more females) is considered more natural and tends to reduce stress on the single male.

Numerous caves and hiding places are generally advised, reflecting its natural habitat on deep, steep reef slopes.

A single specimen may be housed in somewhat smaller systems (around 200 L), though group keeping is considered more appropriate.

Keeping the upper temperature bound closer to 26–27°C may be appropriate for specimens displaying stress at lower temperatures.

Taxonomy note: this species was reclassified from the family Serranidae into Anthiadidae, and is currently recognized under the scientific name Pyronotanthias lori (Lubbock & Randall, 1976). The older name Pseudanthias lori is still widely used in the aquarium trade.

This species was described approximately 25 years before its trade availability and is sourced primarily from Cook Islands and Fiji. It has been reported to be harvested from relatively deep water (30–60 m), which may contribute to the need for acclimation care on arrival.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered difficult. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is removed or lost. Specific captive breeding records for this species are not well documented.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm max), temperature range (22–27°C), native range (Indo-Pacific, Christmas Island to Tuamotu Islands, north to Japan, south to northern GBR), taxonomy (Anthiadidae / Perciformes), and habitat (reef drop-offs 30–60 m). FishBase uses the updated name Pyronotanthias lori; LiveAquaria uses Pseudanthias lori. FishBase also lists the preferred temp range as 25.3–29°C, higher than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); averaged upper bound used (26°C). Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo did not have a page for this species; Fishkeeping World general anthias page returned 404; TFH Magazine returned 404 — FishBase is the confirmed secondary.

fish.breedingNotes: Lori's Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite in which sex change occurs socially — if the dominant male is lost, the most dominant female will transition to take its place. No well-documented captive breeding records are available for this species.
